summary: The q8 dataset shows uniform activity across all ten problems (q1-q10), each with exactly 100 recorded issues, indicating either consistent engagement or a flat, possibly placeholder-driven data state. No problem stands out by issue volume, and the absence of differentiated proof status or resolution metadata limits confidence in assessing genuine traction or solvability.
problems: All problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 issues, suggesting the count may be capped, aggregated uniformly, or not yet meaningfully differentiated.
Proof status is listed only as issue counts with no breakdown of open vs. resolved, blocking assessment of actual progress.
No data on issue type, severity, or recency is available, so common patterns and near-term solvability cannot be reliably inferred.
Lack of per-problem differentiation prevents identification of which problems have real momentum versus stalled effort.
highlights: Comprehensive coverage: every problem in the dataset has tracked issues, indicating no problem is being neglected.
Consistent data collection across all ten problems provides a uniform baseline for future comparative analysis.
The structured issue-tracking framework is in place, ready to surface insights once richer metadata is added.

suggested_todos.title: Augment issue data with open/resolved status and severity to distinguish real progress per problem
Investigate why all problems show exactly 100 issues to rule out a data cap or ingestion artifact
Add timestamps and activity recency to identify which problems have current traction
Tag issues by category (e.g., lemma gaps, notation, logic errors) to reveal common patterns
Define solvability indicators so near-term-closable problems can be prioritized
